nativeRange.habitat: Shallow protected coral reef environments, silty sand and rubble bottoms, lagoons and inner reef slopes typically at depths of 0–10 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates in the wild, including small crustaceans, molluscs and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality marine pellets or flakes. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day.

summary: The Green Wrasse (Halichoeres chloropterus) is an Indo-Pacific reef species that develops a pastel green body and intricate reticulate facial markings in the male phase. Generally considered one of the more peaceful wrasses in community reef aquariums, it is best regarded as reef-safe with caution, as it will prey on ornamental shrimp and small invertebrates.
careNotes: Best housed in a well-established saltwater aquarium with abundant live rock for foraging and refuge, open swimming space, and a fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep — the species buries itself in the sand at night and when startled, so a coarse or thin substrate is generally considered unsuitable. A tightly fitting lid is widely recommended, as Halichoeres wrasses have been known to jump from open-top tanks. Generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, though older, larger specimens have been reported to show aggression toward smaller, more passive fish (including other wrasses) and toward conspecifics; best kept singly, as a male/female pair, or as a small harem of one male with several females. Considered reef-safe with caution — it does not directly harm corals or clams and has been reported to help control flatworms and pyramidellid snails, but it cannot distinguish between desirable and undesirable invertebrates and will tend to consume ornamental shrimp, fan worms, and other small crustaceans. Like most Halichoeres species, the Green Wrasse is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, with juveniles and females showing dark dots that gradually fade as mature males develop the green base colour and reticulate head pattern.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquaria. In the wild the species pairs during spawning and is a protogynous hermaphrodite.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Original wrasse-reef-safe collection URL did not contain a dedicated Green Wrasse page; swapped to the LiveAquaria species product page found via search. Care level, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters (temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 8" max size, 75 US gal minimum tank, sand-burrowing and tight-lid notes.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked 75 US gal minimum tank, 8" adult size, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, KH 8–12), reef-safe rating, jumping risk and need for tight-fitting lid, predation on red planaria, fireworms and pyramidellid snails, recommendation to add before larger/more aggressive tankmates.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females can transition to male under social pressure.
